g.data-narration: Framing is how the Data Link Layer organizes raw bits from the Physical Layer into identifiable frames with clear boundaries.
g.text: Understanding Frame Structure, Bit Stuffing, and Byte Stuffing

g.data-narration: The Physical Layer only transmits raw bits without structure. Without framing, receivers cannot identify where messages begin or end.
A frame has three main parts: Header with addresses and control info, Payload carrying network layer data, and Trailer with error detection.
Character-oriented framing uses special flag bytes (0x7E) to mark frame boundaries. When flags appear in data, escape characters (0x7D) handle them.
Bit-oriented protocols treat frames as bit streams, using flag 01111110. Bit stuffing inserts a 0 after five consecutive 1s for transparency.
Let's apply bit stuffing to the sequence with two instances of five consecutive 1s. We insert a 0 after each occurrence.
For byte stuffing example: payload containing flag (0x7E) and escape (0x7D) characters requires escape sequences in the transmitted data.
Ethernet frames don't use bit/byte stuffing. They use physical layer preamble and Start Frame Delimiter for synchronization.
To analyze Ethernet frames, identify the 6-byte destination and source MAC addresses in the header from the hex dump.
g.text: AA AA AA AA AA AA 00 0C 29 4A 5B 3E 08 00 45 00 00 34...

g.data-narration: Frame synchronization identifies frame boundaries, while transparency ensures any data pattern can be transmitted without confusion.
g.text: Any data pattern can be transmitted
⚠ Loss of sync → frame slippage or communication failure

g.data-narration: Some protocols use physical layer coding violations as frame delimiters, eliminating the need for stuffing operations entirely.
g.text: Special symbols that don't represent valid data

g.data-narration: Compare framing methods: HDLC/PPP use bit stuffing, PPP async uses byte stuffing, Ethernet uses physical layer mechanisms.
Key points: Framing adds structure to raw bits. Bit stuffing is more efficient than byte stuffing. Ethernet doesn't use stuffing.
g.text: • Framing groups raw bits into identifiable frames with header, payload, and trailer
• Bit stuffing: insert '0' after five '1's (flag: 01111110) - more efficient
• Byte stuffing: escape 0x7E→0x7D 0x5E, 0x7D→0x7D 0x5D - used in async links
• Ethernet uses NO stuffing - relies on preamble/SFD and physical layer coding

g.data-narration: Remember: destuffing happens BEFORE FCS calculation. Minimum Ethernet frame is 64 bytes for collision detection.
g.text: ✓ Destuffing occurs BEFORE FCS calculation at receiver
✓ FCS uses CRC for error detection
